title: Great Recession

infobox.value: *(disputed)* * US [housing](/wiki/Housing_in_the_United_States "Housing in the United States") policy * [Real-estate bubbles](/wiki/Real-estate_bubble "Real-estate bubble") bursting * Limited [financial regulation](/wiki/Financial_regulation "Financial regulation")

infobox.key: Part of a series on the

infobox.value: | * [v](/wiki/Template:Great_Recession_series "Template:Great Recession series") * [t](/wiki/Template_talk:Great_Recession_series "Template talk:Great Recession series") * [e](/wiki/Special:EditPage/Template:Great_Recession_series "Special:EditPage/Template:Great Recession series")

was a period of market decline in economies around the world, particularly in the [western world](/wiki/Western_world "Western world") and surrounding countries, that occurred from late 2007 to mid-2009,[[1]](#cite_note-NBRE_dates-1) overlapping with the closely related [2008 financial crisis](/wiki/2008_financial_crisis "2008 financial crisis"). The scale and timing of the [recession](/wiki/Recession "Recession") varied from country to country (see map).[[2]](#cite_note-DPAD121912-2)[[3]](#cite_note-UN011513-3) At the time, the [International Monetary Fund](/wiki/International_Monetary_Fund "International Monetary Fund") (IMF) concluded that it was the most severe economic and financial meltdown since the [Great Depression](/wiki/Great_Depression "Great Depression").

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

2007–2009 international economic decline

For background on financial market events beginning in 2007, see [2008 financial crisis](/wiki/2008_financial_crisis "2008 financial crisis"). For the 2020 recession, see [COVID-19 recession](/wiki/COVID-19_recession "COVID-19 recession").

Not to be confused with [Wall Street crash of 1929](/wiki/Wall_Street_crash_of_1929 "Wall Street crash of 1929") or [Great Resignation](/wiki/Great_Resignation "Great Resignation").

The **Great Recession** was a period of market decline in economies around the world, particularly in the [western world](/wiki/Western_world "Western world") and surrounding countries, that occurred from late 2007 to mid-2009,[[1]](#cite_note-NBRE_dates-1) overlapping with the closely related [2008 financial crisis](/wiki/2008_financial_crisis "2008 financial crisis"). The scale and timing of the [recession](/wiki/Recession "Recession") varied from country to country (see map).[[2]](#cite_note-DPAD121912-2)[[3]](#cite_note-UN011513-3) At the time, the [International Monetary Fund](/wiki/International_Monetary_Fund "International Monetary Fund") (IMF) concluded that it was the most severe economic and financial meltdown since the [Great Depression](/wiki/Great_Depression "Great Depression").

The Great Recession was caused by many weaknesses that slowly developed in the financial system, along with a series of triggering events that began with the bursting of the United States [housing bubble](/wiki/2000s_United_States_housing_bubble "2000s United States housing bubble") in 2005–2012. When housing prices fell and homeowners began to abandon their mortgages, the value of [mortgage-backed securities](/wiki/Mortgage-backed_securities "Mortgage-backed securities") held by investment banks declined in 2007–2008, causing several to collapse or be bailed out in September 2008. This 2007–2008 phase was called the [subprime mortgage crisis](/wiki/Subprime_mortgage_crisis "Subprime mortgage crisis").

The combination of banks being unable to provide funds to businesses and homeowners paying down debt rather than borrowing and spending resulted in the Great Recession. The recession officially began in the U.S. in December 2007 and lasted until June 2009, thus extending over 19 months.[[4]](#cite_note-BernankeFCIC1-4)[[5]](#cite_note-nber.org-5) As with most other recessions, it appears that no known formal theoretical or empirical model was able to accurately predict the advance of this recession, except for minor signals in the sudden rise of forecast probabilities, which were still well under 50%.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

The recession was not felt equally around the world; whereas most of the world's [developed economies](/wiki/Developed_economies "Developed economies"), particularly in North America, South America and Europe, fell into a severe, sustained recession, many more recently developing economies suffered far less impact, particularly [China](/wiki/Economy_of_China "Economy of China"), [India](/wiki/Economy_of_India "Economy of India") and [Indonesia](/wiki/Economy_of_Indonesia "Economy of Indonesia"), whose [economies grew](/wiki/Economic_growth "Economic growth") substantially during this period. Similarly, [Oceania](/wiki/Oceania "Oceania") suffered [minimal impact](/wiki/Great_Recession_in_Oceania "Great Recession in Oceania"), in part due to its proximity to Asian markets.

## Terminology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Great_Recession&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Terminology")]

Two [definitions](/wiki/Semantics "Semantics") of the term "economic recession" exist: one sense referring generally to "a period of reduced economic activity"[[7]](#cite_note-Merriam-Webster_recession-7) and ongoing hardship; and a technical definition used in [economics](/wiki/Economics "Economics"), which is [defined operationally](/wiki/Operational_definition "Operational definition"), specifically the [contraction phase](/wiki/Inflection_point "Inflection point") of a [business cycle](/wiki/Business_cycle "Business cycle") with two or more consecutive quarters of [GDP](/wiki/GDP "GDP") contraction (negative GDP growth rate). The latter is typically used to influence abrupt changes in monetary policy.

Under the technical definition, the recession ended in the United States in June or July 2009.[[8]](#cite_note-8)[[9]](#cite_note-9)[[10]](#cite_note-10)[[11]](#cite_note-11)

Journalist [Robert Kuttner](/wiki/Robert_Kuttner "Robert Kuttner") has argued that 'The Great Recession' is a misnomer. According to Kuttner, "recessions are mild dips in the business cycle that are either self-correcting or soon cured by modest fiscal or monetary stimulus. Because of the continuing deflationary trap, it would be more accurate to call this decade's stagnant economy The Lesser Depression or The Great Deflation."[[12]](#cite_note-12)

## Overview

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Great_Recession&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Overview")]

The Great Recession met the [IMF](/wiki/IMF "IMF") criteria for being a [global recession](/wiki/Global_recession "Global recession") only in the single calendar year 2009.[[13]](#cite_note-New_IMF_Global_Recession_definition-13)[[14]](#cite_note-IMF_WEO_2009-14) That IMF definition requires a *decline in annual real [world GDP](/wiki/Gross_world_product "Gross world product") per capita*. Despite the fact that quarterly data are being used as [recession](/wiki/Recession "Recession") definition criteria by all [G20 members](/wiki/G20 "G20"), representing 85% of the [world GDP](/wiki/Gross_world_product "Gross world product"),[[15]](#cite_note-15) the International Monetary Fund (IMF) has decided – in the absence of a complete data set – not to declare/measure global recessions according to quarterly GDP data. The *seasonally adjusted [PPP](/wiki/Purchasing_power_parity "Purchasing power parity")‑weighted real GDP* for the G20‑zone, however, is a good indicator for the world GDP, and it was measured to have suffered a direct [quarter on quarter](/wiki/Quarter_on_quarter "Quarter on quarter") decline during the three quarters from Q3‑2008 until Q1‑2009, which more accurately mark when the recession took place at the global level.[[16]](#cite_note-stats.oecd.org-16)

According to the U.S. [National Bureau of Economic Research](/wiki/National_Bureau_of_Economic_Research "National Bureau of Economic Research") (the official arbiter of U.S. recessions), the recession began in December 2007 and ended in June 2009, and thus extended over eighteen months.[[5]](#cite_note-nber.org-5)[[17]](#cite_note-17)

[![A number of people queuing at the door of a branch of the Northern Rock bank.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b4/Northern_Rock_Queue.jpg/250px-Northern_Rock_Queue.jpg)](/wiki/File:Northern_Rock_Queue.jpg)

A [bank run](/wiki/Bank_run "Bank run") at a branch of the [Northern Rock](/wiki/Northern_Rock "Northern Rock") bank in [Brighton](/wiki/Brighton "Brighton"), England, on September 14, 2007, amid speculation of problems, prior to its [2008 nationalisation](/wiki/Nationalisation_of_Northern_Rock "Nationalisation of Northern Rock")

The years leading up to the crisis were characterized by an exorbitant rise in asset prices and associated boom in economic demand.[[18]](#cite_note-18) Further, the U.S. [shadow banking system](/wiki/Shadow_banking_system "Shadow banking system") (i.e., non-depository financial institutions such as investment banks) had grown to rival the depository system yet was not subject to the same regulatory oversight, making it vulnerable to a [bank run](/wiki/Bank_run "Bank run").[[19]](#cite_note-19)

U.S. [mortgage-backed securities](/wiki/Mortgage-backed_securities "Mortgage-backed securities"), which had risks that were hard to assess, were marketed around the world, as they offered higher yields than U.S. government bonds. Many of these securities were backed by subprime mortgages, which collapsed in value when the U.S. housing bubble burst during 2006 and homeowners began to default on their mortgage payments in large numbers starting in 2007.[[20]](#cite_note-20)

The emergence of [subprime loan](/wiki/Subprime_loan "Subprime loan") losses in 2007 began the crisis and exposed other risky loans and over-inflated asset prices. With loan losses mounting and the fall of [Lehman Brothers](/wiki/Lehman_Brothers "Lehman Brothers") on September 15, 2008, a major panic broke out on the inter-bank loan market. There was the equivalent of a bank run on the [shadow banking system](/wiki/Shadow_banking_system "Shadow banking system"), resulting in many large and well established [investment banks](/wiki/Investment_bank "Investment bank") and [commercial banks](/wiki/Commercial_bank "Commercial bank") in the United States and [Europe](/wiki/Europe "Europe") suffering huge losses and even facing bankruptcy, resulting in massive public financial assistance (government bailouts).[[21]](#cite_note-KrugmanGeithner1-21)

The global recession that followed resulted in a sharp drop in [international trade](/wiki/International_trade "International trade"), rising [unemployment](/wiki/Unemployment "Unemployment") and slumping commodity prices.[[22]](#cite_note-22) Several economists predicted that recovery might not appear until 2011 and that the recession would be the worst since the [Great Depression](/wiki/Great_Depression "Great Depression") of the 1930s.[[23]](#cite_note-23)[[24]](#cite_note-24) Economist [Paul Krugman](/wiki/Paul_Krugman "Paul Krugman") once commented on this as seemingly the beginning of "a second Great Depression".[[25]](#cite_note-25)

Governments and central banks responded with [fiscal policy](/wiki/Fiscal_policy "Fiscal policy") and [monetary policy](/wiki/Monetary_policy "Monetary policy") initiatives to stimulate national economies and reduce financial system risks. The recession renewed interest in [Keynesian](/wiki/Keynesian "Keynesian") economic ideas on how to combat recessionary conditions. Economists advise that the stimulus measures such as quantitative easing (pumping money into the system) and holding down central bank wholesale lending interest rates should be withdrawn as soon as economies recover enough to "chart a path to [sustainable growth](/wiki/Sustainable_growth "Sustainable growth")".[[26]](#cite_note-26)[[27]](#cite_note-27)[[28]](#cite_note-28)

The distribution of household incomes in the United States became more unequal during the post-2008 [economic recovery](/wiki/Economic_recovery "Economic recovery").[[29]](#cite_note-Appelbaum-29) [Income inequality in the United States](/wiki/Income_inequality_in_the_United_States "Income inequality in the United States") grew from 2005 to 2012 in more than two thirds of metropolitan areas.[[30]](#cite_note-isChokshi-30) Median household wealth fell 35% in the U.S., from $106,591 to $68,839 between 2005 and 2011.[[31]](#cite_note-Kurtzleben-31)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/de/The_Great_Asset_Bubble.svg/330px-The_Great_Asset_Bubble.svg.png)](/wiki/File:The_Great_Asset_Bubble.svg)

The great asset bubble:[[32]](#cite_note-32)   
**1.** Central banks' gold reserves: $0.845 trillion   
**2.** M0 (paper money): $3.9 trillion   
**3.** Traditional (fractional reserve) banking assets: $39 trillion   
**4.** Shadow banking assets: $62 trillion   
**5.** Other assets: $290 trillion   
**6.** Bail-out money (early 2009): $1.9 trillion

The U.S. [Financial Crisis Inquiry Commission](/wiki/Financial_Crisis_Inquiry_Commission "Financial Crisis Inquiry Commission"), composed of six Democratic and four Republican appointees, reported its majority findings in January 2011. It concluded that "the crisis was avoidable and was caused by:

* Widespread failures in financial regulation, including the Federal Reserve's failure to stem the tide of toxic mortgages;
* Dramatic breakdowns in corporate governance including too many financial firms acting recklessly and taking on too much risk;
* An explosive mix of excessive borrowing and risk by households and Wall Street that put the financial system on a collision course with crisis;
* Key policy makers ill prepared for the crisis, lacking a full understanding of the financial system they oversaw; and systemic breaches in accountability and ethics at all levels."[[33]](#cite_note-stanford1-33)

There were two Republican dissenting FCIC reports. One of them, signed by three Republican appointees, concluded that there were multiple causes. In his separate dissent to the majority and minority opinions of the FCIC, Commissioner [Peter J. Wallison](/wiki/Peter_J._Wallison "Peter J. Wallison") of the [American Enterprise Institute](/wiki/American_Enterprise_Institute "American Enterprise Institute") (AEI) primarily blamed U.S. housing policy, including the actions of [Fannie](/wiki/Fannie_Mae "Fannie Mae") and [Freddie](/wiki/Freddie_Mac "Freddie Mac"), for the crisis. He wrote: "When the bubble began to deflate in mid-2007, the low quality and high risk loans engendered by government policies failed in unprecedented numbers."[[34]](#cite_note-34)

In its "Declaration of the Summit on Financial Markets and the World Economy," dated November 15, 2008, leaders of the [Group of 20](/wiki/Group_of_20 "Group of 20") cited the following causes:

> During a period of strong global growth, growing capital flows, and prolonged stability earlier this decade, market participants sought higher yields without an adequate appreciation of the risks and failed to exercise proper due diligence. At the same time, weak underwriting standards, unsound risk management practices, increasingly complex and opaque financial products, and consequent excessive leverage combined to create vulnerabilities in the system. Policy-makers, regulators and supervisors, in some advanced countries, did not adequately appreciate and address the risks building up in financial markets, keep pace with financial innovation, or take into account the systemic ramifications of domestic regulatory actions.[[35]](#cite_note-35)

Federal Reserve Chair [Ben Bernanke](/wiki/Ben_Bernanke "Ben Bernanke") testified in September 2010 before the FCIC regarding the causes of the crisis. He wrote that there were shocks or triggers (i.e., particular events that touched off the crisis) and vulnerabilities (i.e., structural weaknesses in the financial system, regulation and supervision) that amplified the shocks. Examples of triggers included: losses on subprime mortgage securities that began in 2007 and a [run](/wiki/Bank_run "Bank run") on the [shadow banking system](/wiki/Shadow_banking_system "Shadow banking system") that began in the middle of 2007, which adversely affected the functioning of money markets. Examples of vulnerabilities in the *private* sector included: financial institution dependence on unstable sources of short-term funding such as [repurchase agreements](/wiki/Repurchase_agreements "Repurchase agreements") or Repos; deficiencies in corporate risk management; excessive use of leverage (borrowing to invest); and inappropriate usage of derivatives as a tool for taking excessive risks. Examples of vulnerabilities in the *public* sector included: statutory gaps and conflicts between regulators; ineffective use of regulatory authority; and ineffective crisis management capabilities. Bernanke also discussed "[Too big to fail](/wiki/Too_big_to_fail "Too big to fail")" institutions, monetary policy, and trade deficits.[[4]](#cite_note-BernankeFCIC1-4)

### Narratives

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Great_Recession&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Narratives")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5e/U.S._Fixed_Investment_as_Pct_GDP.png/500px-U.S._Fixed_Investment_as_Pct_GDP.png)](/wiki/File:U.S._Fixed_Investment_as_Pct_GDP.png)

U.S. residential and non-residential investment fell relative to GDP during the crisis.

There are several "narratives" attempting to place the causes of the recession into context, with overlapping elements. Five such narratives include:

1. There was the equivalent of a [bank run](/wiki/Bank_run "Bank run") on the [shadow banking system](/wiki/Shadow_banking_system "Shadow banking system"), which includes investment banks and other non-depository financial entities. This system had grown to rival the depository system in scale yet was not subject to the same regulatory safeguards. Its failure disrupted the flow of credit to consumers and corporations.[[21]](#cite_note-KrugmanGeithner1-21)[[36]](#cite_note-frbatlanta.org-36)
2. The [U.S. economy](/wiki/U.S._economy "U.S. economy") was being driven by a housing bubble. When it burst, private residential investment (i.e., housing construction) fell by over four percent of GDP.[[37]](#cite_note-37)[[38]](#cite_note-38) Consumption enabled by bubble-generated housing wealth also slowed. This created a gap in annual demand (GDP) of nearly $1 trillion. The U.S. government was unwilling to make up for this private sector shortfall.[[39]](#cite_note-39)[[40]](#cite_note-40)
3. Record levels of [household debt](/wiki/Household_debt "Household debt") accumulated in the decades preceding the crisis resulted in a [balance sheet recession](/wiki/Balance_sheet_recession "Balance sheet recession") (similar to [debt deflation](/wiki/Debt_deflation "Debt deflation")) once housing prices began falling in 2006. Consumers began paying off debt, which reduces their consumption, slowing down the economy for an extended period while debt levels are reduced.[[21]](#cite_note-KrugmanGeithner1-21)[[41]](#cite_note-Mian_and_Sufi2014-41)
4. U.S. government policies encouraged home ownership even for those who could not afford it, contributing to lax lending standards, unsustainable housing price increases, and indebtedness.[[42]](#cite_note-Sowell_2009-42)
5. Wealthy and middle-class [house flippers](/wiki/Flipping "Flipping") with mid-to-good credit scores created a [speculative bubble](/wiki/Speculative_bubble "Speculative bubble") in house prices, and then wrecked local housing markets and financial institutions after they defaulted on their debt en masse.[[43]](#cite_note-43)

Underlying narratives #1–3 is a hypothesis that growing [income inequality](/wiki/Income_inequality_in_the_United_States "Income inequality in the United States") and [wage stagnation](/wiki/Household_income_in_the_United_States "Household income in the United States") encouraged families to increase their [household debt](/wiki/Household_debt "Household debt") to maintain their desired living standard, fueling the bubble. Further, this greater share of income flowing to the top increased the political power of business interests, who used that power to [deregulate](/wiki/Deregulate "Deregulate") or limit regulation of the shadow banking system.[[44]](#cite_note-44)[[45]](#cite_note-45)[[46]](#cite_note-46)

Narrative #5 challenges the popular claim (narrative #4) that subprime borrowers with shoddy credit caused the crisis by buying homes they couldn't afford. This narrative is supported by new research showing that the biggest growth of mortgage debt during the U.S. housing boom came from those with good credit scores in the middle and top of the credit score distribution – and that these borrowers accounted for a disproportionate share of defaults.[[47]](#cite_note-47)

### Trade imbalances and debt bubbles

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Great_Recession&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Trade imbalances and debt bubbles")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e2/US_Private_Debt_to_GDP_by_Sector.png/500px-US_Private_Debt_to_GDP_by_Sector.png)](/wiki/File:US_Private_Debt_to_GDP_by_Sector.png)

U.S. households and financial businesses significantly increased borrowing (leverage) in the years leading up to the crisis.

*[The Economist](/wiki/The_Economist "The Economist")* wrote in July 2012 that the inflow of investment dollars required to fund the U.S. trade deficit was a major cause of the housing bubble and financial crisis: "The trade deficit, less than 1% of GDP in the early 1990s, hit 6% in 2006. That deficit was financed by inflows of foreign savings, in particular from East Asia and the Middle East. Much of that money went into dodgy mortgages to buy overvalued houses, and the financial crisis was the result."[[48]](#cite_note-48)

In May 2008, NPR explained in their [Peabody Award](/wiki/Peabody_Award "Peabody Award") winning program "[The Giant Pool of Money](/wiki/The_Giant_Pool_of_Money "The Giant Pool of Money")" that a vast inflow of savings from developing nations flowed into the mortgage market, driving the U.S. housing bubble. This pool of fixed income savings increased from around $35 trillion in 2000 to about $70 trillion by 2008. NPR explained this money came from various sources, "[b]ut the main headline is that all sorts of poor countries became kind of rich, making things like TVs and selling us oil. China, India, Abu Dhabi, Saudi Arabia made a lot of money and banked it."[[49]](#cite_note-49)

Describing the crisis in Europe, [Paul Krugman](/wiki/Paul_Krugman "Paul Krugman") wrote in February 2012 that: "What we're basically looking at, then, is a balance of payments problem, in which capital flooded south after the creation of the euro, leading to overvaluation in southern Europe."[[50]](#cite_note-50)

### Monetary policy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Great_Recession&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Monetary policy")]

Another narrative about the origin has been focused on the respective parts played by public monetary policy (notably in the US) and by the practices of private financial institutions. In the U.S., mortgage funding was unusually decentralised, opaque, and competitive, and it is believed that competition between lenders for revenue and market share contributed to declining underwriting standards and risky lending.

While Alan Greenspan's role as [Chairman of the Federal Reserve](/wiki/Chairman_of_the_Federal_Reserve "Chairman of the Federal Reserve") has been widely discussed, the main point of controversy remains the lowering of the [Federal funds rate](/wiki/Federal_funds_rate "Federal funds rate") to 1% for more than a year, which, according to [Austrian theorists](/wiki/Austrian_School "Austrian School"), injected huge amounts of "easy" credit-based money into the financial system and created an unsustainable economic boom.[[51]](#cite_note-51) There is an argument that Greenspan's actions in the years 2002–2004 were actually motivated by the need to take the U.S. economy out of the [early 2000s recession](/wiki/Early_2000s_recession "Early 2000s recession") caused by the bursting of the [dot-com bubble](/wiki/Dot-com_bubble "Dot-com bubble"): although by doing so he did not avert the crisis, but only postponed it.[[52]](#cite_note-52)[[53]](#cite_note-53)

### High private debt levels

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Great_Recession&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: High private debt levels")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1e/U.S._Household_Debt_Relative_to_Disposable_Income_and_GDP.png/330px-U.S._Household_Debt_Relative_to_Disposable_Income_and_GDP.png)](/wiki/File:U.S._Household_Debt_Relative_to_Disposable_Income_and_GDP.png)

US household debt relative to disposable income and GDP

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/df/Change_in_household_debt_-_v1.png/330px-Change_in_household_debt_-_v1.png)](/wiki/File:Change_in_household_debt_-_v1.png)

U.S. Changes in Household Debt as a percentage of GDP for 1989–2016. Homeowners paying down debt for 2009–2012 was a headwind to the recovery. Economist [Carmen Reinhart](/wiki/Carmen_Reinhart "Carmen Reinhart") explained that this behavior tends to slow recoveries from financial crises relative to typical recessions.[[54]](#cite_note-54)

Another narrative focuses on high levels of private debt in the US economy. USA [household debt](/wiki/Household_debt "Household debt") as a percentage of annual [disposable personal income](/wiki/Disposable_personal_income "Disposable personal income") was 127% at the end of 2007, versus 77% in 1990.[[55]](#cite_note-The_End_of_the_Affair-55)[[56]](#cite_note-56) Faced with increasing mortgage payments as their adjustable rate mortgage payments increased, households began to default in record numbers, rendering mortgage-backed securities worthless. High private debt levels also impact growth by making recessions deeper and the following recovery weaker.[[57]](#cite_note-57)[[58]](#cite_note-58) [Robert Reich](/wiki/Robert_Reich "Robert Reich") claims the amount of debt in the US economy can be traced to [economic inequality](/wiki/Economic_inequality "Economic inequality"), assuming that middle-class wages remained stagnant while wealth concentrated at the top, and households "pull equity from their homes and overload on debt to maintain living standards".[[59]](#cite_note-huff_inequality-59)

The IMF reported in April 2012: "Household debt soared in the years leading up to the downturn. In advanced economies, during the five years preceding 2007, the ratio of household debt to income rose by an average of 39 percentage points, to 138 percent. In Denmark, Iceland, Ireland, the Netherlands, and Norway, debt peaked at more than 200 percent of household income. A surge in household debt to historic highs also occurred in emerging economies such as Estonia, Hungary, Latvia, and Lithuania. The concurrent boom in both house prices and the stock market meant that household debt relative to assets held broadly stable, which masked households' growing exposure to a sharp fall in asset prices. When house prices declined, leading to the [2008 financial crisis](/wiki/2008_financial_crisis "2008 financial crisis"), many households saw their wealth shrink relative to their debt, and, with less income and more unemployment, found it harder to meet mortgage payments. By the end of 2011, real house prices had fallen from their peak by about 41% in Ireland, 29% in Iceland, 23% in Spain and the United States, and 21% in Denmark. Household defaults, underwater mortgages (where the loan balance exceeds the house value), foreclosures, and fire sales are now endemic to a number of economies. Household [deleveraging](/wiki/Deleveraging "Deleveraging") by paying off debts or defaulting on them has begun in some countries. It has been most pronounced in the United States, where about two-thirds of the debt reduction reflects defaults."[[60]](#cite_note-imf.org-60)[[61]](#cite_note-61)

### Pre-recession warnings

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Great_Recession&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Pre-recession warnings")]

The onset of the economic crisis took most people by surprise. A 2009 paper identifies twelve economists and commentators who, between 2000 and 2006, predicted a recession based on the collapse of the then-booming housing market in the United States:[[62]](#cite_note-Bezemer-62) [Dean Baker](/wiki/Dean_Baker "Dean Baker"), [Wynne Godley](/wiki/Wynne_Godley "Wynne Godley"), [Fred Harrison](/wiki/Fred_Harrison_(author) "Fred Harrison (author)"), [Michael Hudson](/wiki/Michael_Hudson_(economist) "Michael Hudson (economist)"), [Eric Janszen](/w/index.php?title=Eric_Janszen&action=edit&redlink=1 "Eric Janszen (page does not exist)"), [Med Jones](/wiki/Med_Jones "Med Jones")[[63]](#cite_note-63) [Steve Keen](/wiki/Steve_Keen "Steve Keen"), [Jakob Brøchner Madsen](/wiki/Jakob_Br%C3%B8chner_Madsen "Jakob Brøchner Madsen"), Jens Kjaer Sørensen, [Kurt Richebächer](/wiki/Kurt_Richeb%C3%A4cher "Kurt Richebächer"), [Nouriel Roubini](/wiki/Nouriel_Roubini "Nouriel Roubini"), [Peter Schiff](/wiki/Peter_Schiff "Peter Schiff"), and [Robert Shiller](/wiki/Robert_Shiller "Robert Shiller").[[62]](#cite_note-Bezemer-62)[[64]](#cite_note-64)

### Housing bubbles

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Great_Recession&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Housing bubbles")]

Further information: [Real-estate bubble](/wiki/Real-estate_bubble "Real-estate bubble")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/34/FCIC_-_Housing_Bubbles_in_Multiple_Countries_2002-2008.png/330px-FCIC_-_Housing_Bubbles_in_Multiple_Countries_2002-2008.png)](/wiki/File:FCIC_-_Housing_Bubbles_in_Multiple_Countries_2002-2008.png)

Housing price appreciation in selected countries, 2002–2008

By 2007, real estate bubbles were still under way in many parts of the world,[[65]](#cite_note-65) especially in the [United States](/wiki/United_States_housing_bubble "United States housing bubble"), France, the [United Kingdom](/wiki/British_property_bubble "British property bubble"), [Spain](/wiki/2008%E2%80%932014_Spanish_real_estate_crisis "2008–2014 Spanish real estate crisis"), the Netherlands, Australia, the United Arab Emirates, [New Zealand](/wiki/New_Zealand_property_bubble "New Zealand property bubble"), [Ireland](/wiki/Irish_property_bubble "Irish property bubble"), [Poland](/wiki/Polish_property_bubble "Polish property bubble"),[[66]](#cite_note-66) [South Africa](/wiki/South_Africa "South Africa"), [Greece](/wiki/Greece "Greece"), [Bulgaria](/wiki/Bulgaria "Bulgaria"), [Croatia](/wiki/Croatia "Croatia"),[[67]](#cite_note-67) [Norway](/wiki/Norway "Norway"), [Singapore](/wiki/Singapore "Singapore"), [South Korea](/wiki/South_Korea "South Korea"), [Sweden](/wiki/Sweden "Sweden"), [Finland](/wiki/Finland "Finland"), [Argentina](/wiki/Argentina "Argentina"),[[68]](#cite_note-68) the [Baltic states](/wiki/Baltic_states "Baltic states"), [India](/wiki/India "India"), [Romania](/wiki/Romania "Romania"), [Ukraine](/wiki/Ukraine "Ukraine") and [China](/wiki/China "China").[[69]](#cite_note-69) U.S. Federal Reserve Chairman [Alan Greenspan](/wiki/Alan_Greenspan "Alan Greenspan") said in mid-2005 that "at a minimum, there's a little 'froth' [in the U.S. housing market]...it's hard not to see that there are a lot of local bubbles".[[70]](#cite_note-70)

*[The Economist](/wiki/The_Economist "The Economist")*, writing at the same time, went further, saying, "[T]he worldwide rise in house prices is the biggest bubble in history".[[71]](#cite_note-71) Real estate bubbles are (by definition of the word "bubble") followed by a price decrease (also known as a *housing price crash*) that can result in many owners holding [negative equity](/wiki/Negative_equity "Negative equity") (a [mortgage](/wiki/Mortgage "Mortgage") debt higher than the current value of the property).

### Ineffective regulation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Great_Recession&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Ineffective regulation")]

#### Derivatives

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Great_Recession&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Derivatives")]

Several sources have noted the failure of the US government to supervise or even require transparency of the [financial instruments](/wiki/Financial_instruments "Financial instruments") known as [derivatives](/wiki/Derivative_(finance) "Derivative (finance)").[[72]](#cite_note-72)[[73]](#cite_note-devils-73)[[74]](#cite_note-faiola-74) Derivatives such as [credit default swaps](/wiki/Credit_default_swap "Credit default swap") (CDSs) were unregulated or barely regulated. [Michael Lewis](/wiki/Michael_Lewis "Michael Lewis") noted CDSs enabled speculators to stack bets on the same mortgage securities. This is analogous to allowing many persons to buy insurance on the same house. Speculators that bought CDS protection were betting significant mortgage security defaults would occur, while the sellers (such as [AIG](/wiki/AIG "AIG")) bet they would not. An unlimited amount could be wagered on the same housing-related securities, provided buyers and sellers of the CDS could be found.[[75]](#cite_note-75) When massive defaults occurred on underlying mortgage securities, companies like AIG that were selling CDS were unable to perform their side of the obligation and defaulted; U.S. taxpayers paid over $100 billion to global financial institutions to honor AIG obligations, generating considerable outrage.[[76]](#cite_note-Scott_Lanman-76)

A 2008 investigative article in *[The Washington Post](/wiki/The_Washington_Post "The Washington Post")* found leading government officials at the time (Federal Reserve Board Chairman [Alan Greenspan](/wiki/Alan_Greenspan "Alan Greenspan"), Treasury Secretary [Robert Rubin](/wiki/Robert_Rubin "Robert Rubin"), and [SEC](/wiki/U.S._Securities_and_Exchange_Commission "U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission") Chairman [Arthur Levitt](/wiki/Arthur_Levitt "Arthur Levitt")) vehemently opposed any regulation of derivatives. In 1998, [Brooksley E. Born](/wiki/Brooksley_E._Born "Brooksley E. Born"), head of the [Commodity Futures Trading Commission](/wiki/Commodity_Futures_Trading_Commission "Commodity Futures Trading Commission"), put forth a policy paper asking for feedback from regulators, lobbyists, and legislators on the question of whether derivatives should be reported, sold through a central facility, or whether capital requirements should be required of their buyers. Greenspan, Rubin, and Levitt pressured her to withdraw the paper and Greenspan persuaded [Congress](/wiki/United_States_Congress "United States Congress") to pass a resolution preventing CFTC from regulating derivatives for another six months – when Born's term of office would expire.[[73]](#cite_note-devils-73) Ultimately, it was the collapse of a specific kind of derivative, the [mortgage-backed security](/wiki/Mortgage-backed_security "Mortgage-backed security"), that triggered the economic crisis of 2008.[[74]](#cite_note-faiola-74)

#### Shadow banking system

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Great_Recession&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Shadow banking system")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/37/Securitization_Market_Activity.png/330px-Securitization_Market_Activity.png)](/wiki/File:Securitization_Market_Activity.png)

Securitization markets were impaired during the crisis.

Paul Krugman wrote in 2009 that the [run](/wiki/Bank_run "Bank run") on the [shadow banking system](/wiki/Shadow_banking_system "Shadow banking system") was the fundamental cause of the crisis. "As the shadow banking system expanded to rival or even surpass conventional banking in importance, politicians and government officials should have realised that they were re-creating the kind of financial vulnerability that made the Great Depression possible – and they should have responded by extending regulations and the financial safety net to cover these new institutions. Influential figures should have proclaimed a simple rule: anything that does what a bank does, anything that has to be rescued in crises the way banks are, should be regulated like a bank." He referred to this lack of controls as "malign neglect".[[77]](#cite_note-Krugman_2009-77)[[78]](#cite_note-78)

During 2008, three of the largest U.S. investment banks either went bankrupt ([Lehman Brothers](/wiki/Lehman_Brothers "Lehman Brothers")) or were sold at [fire sale](/wiki/Fire_sale "Fire sale") prices to other banks ([Bear Stearns](/wiki/Bear_Stearns "Bear Stearns") and [Merrill Lynch](/wiki/Merrill_Lynch "Merrill Lynch")). The investment banks were not subject to the more stringent regulations applied to depository banks. These failures exacerbated the instability in the global financial system. The remaining two investment banks, [Morgan Stanley](/wiki/Morgan_Stanley "Morgan Stanley") and [Goldman Sachs](/wiki/Goldman_Sachs "Goldman Sachs"), potentially facing failure, opted to become commercial banks, thereby subjecting themselves to more stringent regulation but receiving access to credit via the Federal Reserve.[[79]](#cite_note-79)[[80]](#cite_note-80) Further, [American International Group](/wiki/American_International_Group "American International Group") (AIG) had insured mortgage-backed and other securities but was not required to maintain sufficient reserves to pay its obligations when debtors defaulted on these securities. AIG was contractually required to post additional collateral with many creditors and counter-parties, touching off controversy when over $100 billion of U.S. taxpayer money was paid out to major global financial institutions on behalf of AIG. While this money was legally owed to the banks by AIG (under agreements made via credit default swaps purchased from AIG by the institutions), a number of Congressmen and media members expressed outrage that taxpayer money was used to bail out banks.[[76]](#cite_note-Scott_Lanman-76)

Economist [Gary Gorton](/wiki/Gary_Gorton "Gary Gorton") wrote in May 2009

> Unlike the historical banking panics of the 19th and early 20th centuries, the current banking panic is a wholesale panic, not a retail panic. In the earlier episodes, depositors ran to their banks and demanded cash in exchange for their checking accounts. Unable to meet those demands, the banking system became insolvent. The current panic involved financial firms "running" on other financial firms by not renewing sale and repurchase agreements (repo) or increasing the repo margin ("haircut"), forcing massive deleveraging, and resulting in the banking system being insolvent.[[81]](#cite_note-81)

The [Financial Crisis Inquiry Commission](/wiki/Financial_Crisis_Inquiry_Commission "Financial Crisis Inquiry Commission") reported in January 2011:

> In the early part of the 20th century, we erected a series of protections – the Federal Reserve as a [lender of last resort](/wiki/Lender_of_last_resort "Lender of last resort"), federal deposit insurance, ample regulations – to provide a bulwark against the panics that had regularly plagued America's banking system in the 19th century. Yet, over the past 30-plus years, we permitted the growth of a shadow banking system – opaque and laden with short term debt – that rivaled the size of the traditional banking system. Key components of the market – for example, the multitrillion-dollar repo lending market, off-balance-sheet entities, and the use of over-the-counter derivatives – were hidden from view, without the protections we had constructed to prevent financial meltdowns. We had a 21st-century financial system with 19th-century safeguards.[[33]](#cite_note-stanford1-33)

The [Gramm–Leach–Bliley Act](/wiki/Gramm%E2%80%93Leach%E2%80%93Bliley_Act "Gramm–Leach–Bliley Act") (1999), which reduced the regulation of banks by allowing commercial and investment banks to merge, has also been [blamed](/wiki/Gramm%E2%80%93Leach%E2%80%93Bliley_Act#Controversy "Gramm–Leach–Bliley Act") for the crisis, by [Nobel Prize](/wiki/Nobel_Memorial_Prize_in_Economic_Sciences "Nobel Memorial Prize in Economic Sciences")–winning economist [Joseph Stiglitz](/wiki/Joseph_Stiglitz "Joseph Stiglitz") among others.[[82]](#cite_note-82)

### Regulations encouraging lax lending standards

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Great_Recession&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Regulations encouraging lax lending standards")]

Peter Wallison and Edward Pinto of the [American Enterprise Institute](/wiki/American_Enterprise_Institute "American Enterprise Institute"), which advocates for private enterprise and limited government, have asserted that private lenders were encouraged to relax lending standards by government affordable housing policies.[[83]](#cite_note-83)[[84]](#cite_note-84) They cite The Housing and Community Development Act of 1992, which initially required that 30 percent or more of Fannie's and Freddie's loan purchases be related to affordable housing. The legislation gave HUD the power to set future requirements. These rose to 42 percent in 1995 and 50 percent in 2000, and by 2008 a 56 percent minimum was established.[[85]](#cite_note-Wallison_1-85)

However, the Financial Crisis Inquiry Commission (FCIC) Democratic majority report concluded that Fannie & Freddie "were not a primary cause" of the crisis and that CRA was not a factor in the crisis.[[33]](#cite_note-stanford1-33) Further, since housing bubbles appeared in multiple countries in Europe as well, the FCIC Republican minority dissenting report also concluded that U.S. housing policies were not a robust explanation for a wider global housing bubble.[[33]](#cite_note-stanford1-33) The hypothesis that a primary cause of the crisis was U.S. government housing policy requiring banks to make risky loans has been widely disputed,[[86]](#cite_note-86) with [Paul Krugman](/wiki/Paul_Krugman "Paul Krugman") referring to it as "imaginary history".[[87]](#cite_note-87)

One of the other challenges with blaming government regulations for essentially forcing banks to make risky loans is the timing. Subprime lending increased from around 10% of mortgage origination historically to about 20% only from 2004 to 2006, with housing prices peaking in 2006. Blaming affordable housing regulations established in the 1990s for a sudden spike in subprime origination is problematic at best.[[33]](#cite_note-stanford1-33) A more proximate government action to the sudden rise in subprime lending was the SEC relaxing lending standards for the top investment banks during an April 2004 meeting with bank leaders. These banks increased their risk-taking shortly thereafter, significantly increasing their purchases and securitization of lower-quality mortgages, thus encouraging additional subprime and Alt-A lending by mortgage companies.[[88]](#cite_note-88) This action by its investment bank competitors also resulted in Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac taking on more risk.[[89]](#cite_note-89)

### Systemic crisis

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Great_Recession&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Systemic crisis")]

The [2008 financial crisis](/wiki/2008_financial_crisis "2008 financial crisis") and the Great Recession were described as a symptom of another, deeper crisis by a number of economists. For example, [Ravi Batra](/wiki/Ravi_Batra "Ravi Batra") argues that growing inequality of [financial capitalism](/wiki/Financial_capitalism "Financial capitalism") produces speculative bubbles that burst and result in depression and major [political changes](/wiki/The_Downfall_of_Capitalism_and_Communism "The Downfall of Capitalism and Communism").[[90]](#cite_note-90)[[91]](#cite_note-91) Feminist economists [Ailsa McKay](/wiki/Ailsa_McKay "Ailsa McKay") and [Margunn Bjørnholt](/wiki/Margunn_Bj%C3%B8rnholt "Margunn Bjørnholt") argue that the [2008 financial crisis](/wiki/2008_financial_crisis "2008 financial crisis") and the response to it revealed a crisis of ideas in mainstream economics and within the economics profession, and call for a reshaping of both the economy, economic theory and the economics profession. They argue that such a reshaping should include new advances within [feminist economics](/wiki/Feminist_economics "Feminist economics") and [ecological economics](/wiki/Ecological_economics "Ecological economics") that take as their starting point the socially responsible, sensible and accountable subject in creating an economy and economic theories that fully acknowledge care for each other as well as the planet.[[92]](#cite_note-92)
